q: A camera is an optical instrument that forms and records an image of an object.
The image formed in a camera may be recorded on film or detected by an electronic sensor.
A diaphragm is placed in front of the convex lens to control the amount of light energy falling on the film.
The aperture is a circular opening through which the light passes.
The f-number of a camera is the ratio of the focal length of the lens to the diameter of aperture.
The smaller the f-number, the wider the aperture is.
When taking a photograph in bright light a small f-number must be used.
A shutter controls the time during which light enters the camera through the lens.
The shorter the exposure time, the lower the shutter speed is.
A camera consists of a focusing ring by which the distance between the lens and the film can be adjusted to obtain a sharp image on the film.
The film at the back of the camera is a screen which receives the image.
Chemical reaction occurs when light energy falls on the film.
The image formed by a camera lens is real and inverted.
For a distant object, its image is formed at the focus of the lens.
